title: Rheumatic Fever
oneLiner: Acute autoimmune reaction to Group A Streptococcal pharyngeal infection causing polyarthritis, carditis, chorea, and long-term rheumatic heart disease; rare in UK/IE but high risk in immigrants and outbreaks.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute severe carditis with heart failure** – look for: gallop rhythm, hepatomegaly, raised JVP, basal crackles, new murmur (mitral regurgitation most common). This is a medical emergency; treat as myocarditis.
- **Bacterial endocarditis** – differentiate by: fever >39°C, splinter haemorrhages, positive blood cultures, and vegetation on echo. Never assume all murmurs are rheumatic.
- **Septic arthritis** – hot, swollen, single joint with high fever and raised CRP; joint aspiration mandatory if monoarticular, even if other joints involved.
sections.Presentation:
- **Arthritis** (60-80%): migratory polyarthritis of large joints (knees, ankles, wrists, elbows). Pain out of proportion to swelling. Each joint inflamed for 1-5 days.
- **Carditis** (50-60%): chest pain, dyspnoea, palpitations, new murmur (most commonly mitral regurgitation, less often aortic). Pericardial rub may be present.
- **Chorea** (Sydenham chorea – 10-30%): involuntary, jerky movements of face, hands, and feet; emotional lability; worse with stress.
- **Subcutaneous nodules**: painless, mobile nodules over extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, occiput). Rare now.
- **Erythema marginatum**: evanescent pink rash with clear centre, non-pruritic, on trunk and proximal limbs, not face.
- **Key exam findings**: fever (≥38.5°C), tachycardia out of proportion to fever, new murmur (mitral regurgitation: pansystolic at apex, radiating to axilla; aortic regurgitation: early diastolic at left sternal edge).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Juvenile idiopathic arthritis** – persistent >6 weeks, no carditis, antinuclear antibody (ANA) positive.
- **Post-streptococcal reactive arthritis** – arthritis but not migratory, no carditis, no chorea, no nodules. **Splitter:** presence of carditis or chorea = rheumatic fever; absence = post-streptococcal reactive arthritis.
- **Bacterial endocarditis** – sustained bacteraemia, pathological murmur, positive blood cultures, vegetation on echo.
- **Lyme disease** – erythema chronicum migrans, unilateral facial palsy, exposure to ticks, serology positive.
- **Systemic lupus erythematosus** – malar rash, oral ulcers, renal involvement, positive anti-dsDNA.
- **Kawasaki disease** – conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, perineal rash, cervical lymphadenopathy; coronary aneurysm on echo.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Fever chart, ECG (look for PR prolongation, QTc prolongation, or signs of pericarditis), echocardiography (transthoracic – all patients suspected of carditis).
- **Bloods:** FBC, CRP, ESR, blood cultures (two sets before antibiotics), anti-streptolysin O titre (ASOT) and anti-DNase B (throat swab for GAS culture if still symptomatic).
- **Imaging:** Chest X-ray – cardiomegaly or signs of heart failure if carditis severe.
- **Special tests:** ECG monitoring if carditis (troponin if suspected myocarditis).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Revised Jones criteria (2015)** – requires evidence of recent GAS infection (˃2 weeks) plus ≥2 major OR 1 major + 2 minor. Major: carditis, polyarthritis, chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules. Minor: fever, arthralgia, raised ESR/CRP, prolonged PR interval.
- **Never-miss test:** **Echocardiogram** in any patient with new murmur, chest pain, or dyspnoea – delay can miss acute carditis and delay steroid therapy.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If carditis present: admit to cardiology / acute medical unit. Monitor ECG, pulse oximetry. Start IV access.
  - Analgesia for arthritis: paracetamol or ibuprofen [check local policy].
  - If septic arthritis suspected: urgent orthopaedic review and joint aspiration.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Eradicate GAS: phenoxymethylpenicillin 250mg QDS PO for 10 days (or azithromycin if penicillin allergic). Do not wait for ASOT results – start immediately.
  - Anti-inflammatory therapy: if arthritis only – aspirin 60-80 mg/kg/day PO in 4 divided doses (max 4 g/day); if carditis – prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg/day PO for 2 weeks then taper. [Check local policy for exact doses].
  - Supportive care: bed rest if active carditis; monitor fluid balance; avoid NSAIDs if heart failure.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Secondary prophylaxis: benzathine penicillin G 1.2 million units IM every 3-4 weeks (or phenoxymethylpenicillin 250mg BD PO if no carditis). Duration: 5 years after last episode or until age 21 (whichever longer); if severe carditis (mitral/aortic disease) – lifelong or until prosthetic valve surgery.
  - Annual dental prophylaxis: counsel on dental health; no routine antibiotics for dental procedures unless per BNF.
  - If residual valvular disease: echocardiogram every 1-2 years; surgical referral if symptomatic (mitral stenosis, regurgitation).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance rate if NBM, otherwise oral.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if weight >50kg, no high bleeding risk).
  - Diet: normal (except if dysphagia from chorea or heart failure).
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, escalate if ≥5 or any new cardiovascular signs.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - Penicillin V (phenoxymethylpenicillin) 250mg PO QDS for 10 days.
  - Benzathine penicillin G 1.2 million units IM (deep buttock) every 3-4 weeks for secondary prophylaxis.
  - Aspirin (for arthritis) 60-80 mg/kg/day PO in divided doses (max 4 g/day) – monitor tinnitus, bleeding; check salicylate levels.
  - Prednisolone (for carditis) 1-2 mg/kg/day PO for 2 weeks then taper over 2-4 weeks.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Azithromycin (penicillin allergy) 500mg PO on day 1 then 250mg OD for 4 days.
  - Ibuprofen (if aspirin contraindicated) 10 mg/kg/dose TDS PO.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Aspirin: avoid in patients <16 years with chickenpox or influenza (Reye's risk); avoid if active GI bleeding.
  - Warfarin + aspirin: increased bleeding risk – avoid combination unless necessary.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No major changes for short courses, but aspirin should be avoided in severe renal impairment (cockcroft-gault <30 ml/min).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Benzathine penicillin** – do not give IV (only deep IM). Also, ensure correct dose and formulation (1.2 million units = 1.8 g) – giving wrong strength or IV can cause cardiorespiratory arrest.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Acute rheumatic carditis leading to heart failure, arrhythmias (including atrial fibrillation), pericardial effusion; Sydenham chorea can cause falls, exhaustion.
- **Chronic:** Rheumatic heart disease (mitral stenosis, mitral regurgitation, aortic regurgitation) – can cause heart failure, atrial fibrillation, stroke, infective endocarditis. Valve surgery may be required.
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality <1% if treated early; acute carditis resolves in 70-80% of patients. However, 50% of patients with acute carditis develop chronic RHD. Recurrence rate 10-50% without secondary prophylaxis. Chorea usually resolves in 1-6 months.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always ask about **recent sore throat** (2-6 weeks prior) and check **throat swab** even if throat asymptomatic.
- Perform a **full cardiovascular exam daily** while on ward – listen for new murmurs, gallop, S3, S4.
- **Start secondary prophylaxis before discharge** – prescribe the first dose of benzathine penicillin in hospital.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not use steroids for arthritis alone** – aspirin is first-line; steroids only for carditis.
- **Do not stop anti-inflammatory therapy abruptly** – taper aspirin/steroids to avoid rebound.
- **Do not rely solely on ASOT** – low sensitivity if swab not taken early; use throat swab and anti-DNase B.
- **Do not forget to check pregnancy test** (especially in chorea) – Sydenham chorea may be triggered by oral contraceptives or pregnancy.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New chest pain, breathlessness, palpitations, or syncope (may indicate carditis). Worsening chorea or falls. Fever >38°C or signs of arthritis recurrence.
- **Follow-up:** Cardiology outpatient review within 2-4 weeks. Echocardiogram at 1 month, then annually if valvular disease. GP to coordinate secondary prophylaxis every 3-4 weeks.
sections.Escalation Criteria: - Call senior/ICU if: NEWS2 ≥5 or any single parameter (RR >25, O2 sats <92%, HR >130, systolic BP <90). Acute heart failure: respiratory distress, hypoxia, raised JVP, gallop rhythm – contact cardiology reg. New atrial fibrillation or ECG changes. Suspected anaphylaxis to penicillin. Any sign of carditis requiring steroids: involve cardiology before starting. If chorea causes inability to swallow or injury – reg review for oral motor assessment.

rawMarkdown:
## Rheumatic Fever
**One-liner:** Acute autoimmune reaction to Group A Streptococcal pharyngeal infection causing polyarthritis, carditis, chorea, and long-term rheumatic heart disease; rare in UK/IE but high risk in immigrants and outbreaks.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute severe carditis with heart failure** – look for: gallop rhythm, hepatomegaly, raised JVP, basal crackles, new murmur (mitral regurgitation most common). This is a medical emergency; treat as myocarditis.
- **Bacterial endocarditis** – differentiate by: fever >39°C, splinter haemorrhages, positive blood cultures, and vegetation on echo. Never assume all murmurs are rheumatic.
- **Septic arthritis** – hot, swollen, single joint with high fever and raised CRP; joint aspiration mandatory if monoarticular, even if other joints involved.

### Presentation
- **Arthritis** (60-80%): migratory polyarthritis of large joints (knees, ankles, wrists, elbows). Pain out of proportion to swelling. Each joint inflamed for 1-5 days.
- **Carditis** (50-60%): chest pain, dyspnoea, palpitations, new murmur (most commonly mitral regurgitation, less often aortic). Pericardial rub may be present.
- **Chorea** (Sydenham chorea – 10-30%): involuntary, jerky movements of face, hands, and feet; emotional lability; worse with stress.
- **Subcutaneous nodules**: painless, mobile nodules over extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, occiput). Rare now.
- **Erythema marginatum**: evanescent pink rash with clear centre, non-pruritic, on trunk and proximal limbs, not face.
- **Key exam findings**: fever (≥38.5°C), tachycardia out of proportion to fever, new murmur (mitral regurgitation: pansystolic at apex, radiating to axilla; aortic regurgitation: early diastolic at left sternal edge).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Juvenile idiopathic arthritis** – persistent >6 weeks, no carditis, antinuclear antibody (ANA) positive.
- **Post-streptococcal reactive arthritis** – arthritis but not migratory, no carditis, no chorea, no nodules. **Splitter:** presence of carditis or chorea = rheumatic fever; absence = post-streptococcal reactive arthritis.
- **Bacterial endocarditis** – sustained bacteraemia, pathological murmur, positive blood cultures, vegetation on echo.
- **Lyme disease** – erythema chronicum migrans, unilateral facial palsy, exposure to ticks, serology positive.
- **Systemic lupus erythematosus** – malar rash, oral ulcers, renal involvement, positive anti-dsDNA.
- **Kawasaki disease** – conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, perineal rash, cervical lymphadenopathy; coronary aneurysm on echo.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Fever chart, ECG (look for PR prolongation, QTc prolongation, or signs of pericarditis), echocardiography (transthoracic – all patients suspected of carditis).
- **Bloods:** FBC, CRP, ESR, blood cultures (two sets before antibiotics), anti-streptolysin O titre (ASOT) and anti-DNase B (throat swab for GAS culture if still symptomatic).
- **Imaging:** Chest X-ray – cardiomegaly or signs of heart failure if carditis severe.
- **Special tests:** ECG monitoring if carditis (troponin if suspected myocarditis).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Revised Jones criteria (2015)** – requires evidence of recent GAS infection (˃2 weeks) plus ≥2 major OR 1 major + 2 minor. Major: carditis, polyarthritis, chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules. Minor: fever, arthralgia, raised ESR/CRP, prolonged PR interval.
- **Never-miss test:** **Echocardiogram** in any patient with new murmur, chest pain, or dyspnoea – delay can miss acute carditis and delay steroid therapy.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If carditis present: admit to cardiology / acute medical unit. Monitor ECG, pulse oximetry. Start IV access.
  - Analgesia for arthritis: paracetamol or ibuprofen [check local policy].
  - If septic arthritis suspected: urgent orthopaedic review and joint aspiration.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Eradicate GAS: phenoxymethylpenicillin 250mg QDS PO for 10 days (or azithromycin if penicillin allergic). Do not wait for ASOT results – start immediately.
  - Anti-inflammatory therapy: if arthritis only – aspirin 60-80 mg/kg/day PO in 4 divided doses (max 4 g/day); if carditis – prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg/day PO for 2 weeks then taper. [Check local policy for exact doses].
  - Supportive care: bed rest if active carditis; monitor fluid balance; avoid NSAIDs if heart failure.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Secondary prophylaxis: benzathine penicillin G 1.2 million units IM every 3-4 weeks (or phenoxymethylpenicillin 250mg BD PO if no carditis). Duration: 5 years after last episode or until age 21 (whichever longer); if severe carditis (mitral/aortic disease) – lifelong or until prosthetic valve surgery.
  - Annual dental prophylaxis: counsel on dental health; no routine antibiotics for dental procedures unless per BNF.
  - If residual valvular disease: echocardiogram every 1-2 years; surgical referral if symptomatic (mitral stenosis, regurgitation).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance rate if NBM, otherwise oral.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if weight >50kg, no high bleeding risk).
  - Diet: normal (except if dysphagia from chorea or heart failure).
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, escalate if ≥5 or any new cardiovascular signs.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - Penicillin V (phenoxymethylpenicillin) 250mg PO QDS for 10 days.
  - Benzathine penicillin G 1.2 million units IM (deep buttock) every 3-4 weeks for secondary prophylaxis.
  - Aspirin (for arthritis) 60-80 mg/kg/day PO in divided doses (max 4 g/day) – monitor tinnitus, bleeding; check salicylate levels.
  - Prednisolone (for carditis) 1-2 mg/kg/day PO for 2 weeks then taper over 2-4 weeks.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Azithromycin (penicillin allergy) 500mg PO on day 1 then 250mg OD for 4 days.
  - Ibuprofen (if aspirin contraindicated) 10 mg/kg/dose TDS PO.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Aspirin: avoid in patients <16 years with chickenpox or influenza (Reye's risk); avoid if active GI bleeding.
  - Warfarin + aspirin: increased bleeding risk – avoid combination unless necessary.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No major changes for short courses, but aspirin should be avoided in severe renal impairment (cockcroft-gault <30 ml/min).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Benzathine penicillin** – do not give IV (only deep IM). Also, ensure correct dose and formulation (1.2 million units = 1.8 g) – giving wrong strength or IV can cause cardiorespiratory arrest.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Acute rheumatic carditis leading to heart failure, arrhythmias (including atrial fibrillation), pericardial effusion; Sydenham chorea can cause falls, exhaustion.
- **Chronic:** Rheumatic heart disease (mitral stenosis, mitral regurgitation, aortic regurgitation) – can cause heart failure, atrial fibrillation, stroke, infective endocarditis. Valve surgery may be required.

### Prognosis
- Mortality <1% if treated early; acute carditis resolves in 70-80% of patients. However, 50% of patients with acute carditis develop chronic RHD. Recurrence rate 10-50% without secondary prophylaxis. Chorea usually resolves in 1-6 months.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always ask about **recent sore throat** (2-6 weeks prior) and check **throat swab** even if throat asymptomatic.
- Perform a **full cardiovascular exam daily** while on ward – listen for new murmurs, gallop, S3, S4.
- **Start secondary prophylaxis before discharge** – prescribe the first dose of benzathine penicillin in hospital.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not use steroids for arthritis alone** – aspirin is first-line; steroids only for carditis.
- **Do not stop anti-inflammatory therapy abruptly** – taper aspirin/steroids to avoid rebound.
- **Do not rely solely on ASOT** – low sensitivity if swab not taken early; use throat swab and anti-DNase B.
- **Do not forget to check pregnancy test** (especially in chorea) – Sydenham chorea may be triggered by oral contraceptives or pregnancy.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New chest pain, breathlessness, palpitations, or syncope (may indicate carditis). Worsening chorea or falls. Fever >38°C or signs of arthritis recurrence.
- **Follow-up:** Cardiology outpatient review within 2-4 weeks. Echocardiogram at 1 month, then annually if valvular disease. GP to coordinate secondary prophylaxis every 3-4 weeks.

### Escalation Criteria
- Call senior/ICU if: NEWS2 ≥5 or any single parameter (RR >25, O2 sats <92%, HR >130, systolic BP <90). Acute heart failure: respiratory distress, hypoxia, raised JVP, gallop rhythm – contact cardiology reg. New atrial fibrillation or ECG changes. Suspected anaphylaxis to penicillin. Any sign of carditis requiring steroids: involve cardiology before starting. If chorea causes inability to swallow or injury – reg review for oral motor assessment.
